planting.note: Plant tuberlets or bulbils 2-3 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; pinellia multiplies rapidly by producing abundant bulbils along the leaf petioles and at the stem base and can spread aggressively in ideal conditions.

directions: Pinellia ternata is a small Asian aroid grown in traditional Chinese medicine gardens for its tubers (ban xia), which are used dried and processed as an anti-nausea and antiemetic herb — the raw tubers are toxic and cause intense oral irritation and burning; always handle with care. The plant produces trifoliate leaves on long petioles and a slender, hooded spathe in late spring, followed by a spike of tiny orange-red berries; it grows happily in the deep shade of trees where few other plants thrive and makes a useful, if spreading, shade groundcover in Asian herb gardens. In zone 9, it may behave as a cool-season annual.

planting.note: Plant container plants or start from seed (seeds are tiny; surface-sow in spring on moist medium and do not cover) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; calamint prefers a position sheltered from drying winds and produces the most flowers with a few hours of afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Calamintha grandiflora is the showiest of the calamlints, producing soft, aromatic, mint-scented foliage and large (for the genus), vivid pink, tubular, two-lipped flowers in whorled clusters from late spring through early fall that are extraordinarily attractive to bumblebees and are among the most bumblebee-visited ornamental herbs in British and European garden trials. The leaves and young shoots have a strong, pleasant, minty-oregano flavor and can be used fresh or dried as a seasoning and herbal tea; the flavor is similar to but more delicate than wild mountain oregano.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; catnip is very easy from seed and will self-sow prolifically unless deadheaded, naturalizing readily in garden edges and roadsides.

directions: Nepeta cataria is a bushy, square-stemmed perennial in the mint family with distinctive soft, gray-green, heart-shaped to triangular leaves with a strong, distinctive musky-minty scent, and dense spikes of small, white to pale lavender, purple-spotted flowers from June through September that are excellent bee plants; the nepetalactone compound in the foliage produces the well-known euphoric response in domestic cats, and the plant is used to stuff cat toys and promote play. As an herbal tea, catnip has mild sedative and digestive properties; it has a long history of use for insomnia, anxiety, and infant colic. Cut back hard after the first bloom flush to promote fresh growth and a second flowering.
sources: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health

common_name: Tufted Evening Primrose / White Evening Primrose

planting.note: Direct sow in well-drained to dry, rocky or sandy, poor to average soil in full sun; tufted evening primrose demands sharp drainage and will rot in heavy clay or consistently moist soil.

directions: Oenothera caespitosa is a low-growing western native perennial that forms stemless rosettes of large, silvery-gray, wavy-margined leaves at ground level and produces a succession of enormous (3-4 inch diameter), pure white, four-petaled flowers with bright yellow centers from late spring through midsummer; the flowers open at dusk, last one night, and turn pink as they age the next morning, making this one of the finest plants for a moonlight garden or evening patio. The intensely sweet fragrance is extraordinary at night and attracts sphinx moths (hawkmoths) that are the primary pollinators; it is also a larval host plant for several sphinx moth species. Exceptional for dry rock gardens, wall crevices, and xeriscape plantings in zones 3-7.

planting.note: Plant small corm divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade, setting corms 2-3 inches deep; Carolina spring beauty goes completely dormant by late spring, so mark its position and plant ferns or hostas nearby to fill the gap.

directions: Claytonia caroliniana is nearly identical to its sister species C. virginica (Virginia spring beauty) but produces broader, oval to spatula-shaped leaves rather than the narrow, grass-like leaves of C. virginica; both produce nodding racemes of dainty, pale pink, five-petaled flowers with deeper pink veins in early spring, often emerging through the last snow of the season. The flowers are among the most important early nectar sources for native queen bumblebees and the specialist Andrena carlini bee. The small, starchy corms are edible and were used as a food source by Native Americans; the leaves are also edible as a spring green salad ingredient before the plant flowers.
